findings.title: Snapshots with null marketCap pass the $1B floor silently
findings.description: MIN_MCAP filter at line 822: `if (mcapVal != null && (mcapVal < MIN_MCAP || mcapVal > MAX_MCAP))`. The `mcapVal != null` guard means that when Yahoo returns no marketCap (canonical.marketCap === null or canonical.marketCap.value === null), the filter is skipped entirely and the snapshot is written, regardless of whether the firm is actually a micro-cap or unknown size. Combined with the `_metric` helper that returns null when sd.marketCap is missing, this admits any ticker whose mcap Yahoo doesn't disclose.
findings.mechanism: _metric(_y(sd, 'marketCap'), ...) returns null when sd.marketCap is missing/non-finite. canonical.marketCap is then null, mcapVal evaluates to null, the filter branch is skipped, and the snapshot is written + graded. Downstream methods that require marketCap (priceSales-based, EV-based, sector-median lookups) fail to compute or use stale prior-run values.
findings.reproducer: Mock yahoo input with summaryDetail.marketCap=null (e.g. recently-spun-off ticker before Yahoo populates mcap). mapYahooToCanonical returns canonical.marketCap=null. The line-822 guard short-circuits. The snapshot is written and graded — but mcap is missing, so the universe silently includes a possibly sub-$1B firm.
findings.impact: Universe pollution by tickers with unknown size; downstream sector-median calculations skip these (good) but they still consume a worker slot during the pull and bloat the snapshot directory. Tier rankings can include zero-mcap stubs.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the guard to: `if (mcapVal == null || mcapVal < MIN_MCAP || mcapVal > MAX_MCAP)` — reject snapshots with unknown mcap as if they failed the floor. Or write a meta.mcapUnknown=true flag and have score-aggregator refuse to tier them.
findings.compound_hint: Same null-tolerance pattern appears at line 999 where attempted = n_total - skipped-mcap uses skipped-mcap count for failRatio denominator — silently-passing-null-mcap tickers inflate the denominator and depress the visible fail-ratio.

findings.title: _priceOnlyUpdate's `fxRateApplied || 1` collapses null to identity rate
findings.description: In _priceOnlyUpdate the FX factor for new price/mcap is computed as `const fxApplied = (existing.meta && existing.meta.fxRateApplied) || 1`. When the prior snapshot had `fxConversionFailed=true`, _convertSnapshotToUSD set `fxRateApplied = null` (line 184). The `|| 1` collapses null to 1.0 — meaning the new Yahoo quote (in local currency) is written into `existing.marketCap.value` as if it were USD, with no flag and no skip. The price-only fast-path can therefore silently re-introduce raw local-currency mcap on tickers that the full-pull path would have skipped via fxConversionFailed.
findings.mechanism: Day 0: full pull, currency=ARS, fxConversionFailed=true → snapshot skipped at line 811. BUT if the snapshot already existed from a prior successful FX run (e.g. ARS was supported then dropped), the snapshot file persists. Day N: ticker age < 7d → price-only path runs first (line 689). fxRateApplied is null from a re-graded snapshot, so `|| 1` makes needsFx=false and mcap is overwritten with raw q.marketCap (ARS). Snapshot now mixes USD-denominated annual.* with ARS-denominated marketCap.value.
findings.reproducer: Save a snapshot with meta.fxRateApplied=null, meta.reportingCurrencyOriginal='ARS'. Run pullAll; _priceOnlyUpdate fires. fxApplied = null || 1 = 1. needsFx = (1 !== 1) && ... = false. existing.marketCap.value = q.marketCap (ARS, not multiplied). The snapshot is now corrupt: marketCap in ARS, annual in whatever was last written.
findings.impact: Silent currency corruption on the fast-path for previously-fx-failed tickers. Cross-currency ratio noise. Methods that compare marketCap to revenueTTM (priceSales) produce ratios off by orders of magnitude.
findings.suggested_fix: Change to `const fxApplied = (existing.meta && Number.isFinite(existing.meta.fxRateApplied)) ? existing.meta.fxRateApplied : null;` and refuse the price-only path when fxApplied is null AND reportingCurrencyOriginal !== 'USD'. Falling through to the full pull is safer than silently overwriting with local-currency values.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with the fact that full-pull path skips fxConversionFailed snapshots; the price-only path is the only way these snapshots get refreshed, and it does so incorrectly.

findings.title: loadFx merges stale per-currency rates from FX_FALLBACK even when fx-rates.json is fresh
findings.description: Line 110: `FX_TO_USD = Object.assign({}, FX_FALLBACK, raw.rates)`. This merge means that for any currency present in FX_FALLBACK but MISSING from raw.rates (i.e. refresh-fx.js failed to fetch it this run AND no previous fx-rates.json had it either), the FX_FALLBACK 2024 rate is used silently — even though FX_SOURCE is reported as 'fx-rates.json @ ...' (fresh). The fxRateSource snapshot meta therefore lies: it reads as live but the actual rate used for that specific currency is hardcoded.
findings.mechanism: FX_FALLBACK contains rates for USD/EUR/GBP/CHF/SEK/NOK/DKK/JPY/HKD/CNY/AUD/CAD/KRW/INR/TWD/BRL/MXN/ZAR/SGD with hardcoded 2024 values. If refresh-fx.js succeeded for some but not all of these on the latest run (e.g. INR fetch returned null), the partial fx-rates.json + Object.assign overlay leaves INR=0.012 (2024 value) silently active. snapshot.meta.fxRateSource still says 'fx-rates.json @ ...' because FX_SOURCE is set globally, not per-currency.
findings.reproducer: Backup fx-rates.json. Edit it to remove the 'INR' key from rates. Re-run pull-yahoo on a TCS.NS or INFY.NS ticker. The mapper applies FX_FALLBACK.INR=0.012 but the snapshot meta says fxRateSource='fx-rates.json @ <fresh-timestamp>'.
findings.impact: Per-currency stale-rate detection is impossible — snapshot only knows whether the GLOBAL FX_SOURCE was live or fallback, never which currency was actually overridden. Operators cannot answer 'which tickers had a 2024 JPY rate applied last week?'
findings.suggested_fix: Track the actual per-currency rate source: when FX_TO_USD is built from Object.assign, record FX_RATE_SOURCE[currency] = raw.rates[currency] != null ? 'live' : 'fallback'. In _convertSnapshotToUSD, set snap.meta.fxRateSource based on the specific currency's source, not the global FX_SOURCE. Alternatively, refuse to merge: if fx-rates.json is fresh but lacks a currency, treat the currency as fxConversionFailed.
findings.compound_hint: Cross-check refresh-fx.js's `failed` array: currencies in failed[] are exactly the ones silently filled from FX_FALLBACK. The check-pull-stats.js fxFailed metric is collected but not watched for drift (see F-DQ-009).

findings.title: _arrLen counts {value: null} entries as present, inflating grades for null-aligned FTS arrays
findings.description: Bug #17 fixed NaN entries being counted. But the `_arrLen` filter `return v === null || Number.isFinite(v)` still returns TRUE when v is null — this branch was intended for non-numeric objects (balance rows), but it also matches `{value: null}` wrappers. With the Bug-#26 fix in `_ftsExtractByYear` (pull-yahoo.js line 445) which now PRESERVES null entries for year-alignment, an annualOpInc array of all-null `{value: null}` entries counts at full length, gaming the grade.
findings.mechanism: _ftsExtractByYear returns `[null, null, null, null]` for a field with no data. These nulls land in array slots. When wrapped via subsequent map (or stored directly in canonical.annual.annualSBC/annualCapex), `_arrLen` checks each element: `x == null → false` skips, BUT the check is on `x` not `x.value`. If the element is `{value: null}` (an object), `x == null` is false (object is truthy as `!= null`), then `typeof x === 'number'` false, `x.value` is null so `typeof x.value === 'number'` false → v=null → `v === null` TRUE → counted. Even worse: for `annualOpInc` populated by mapFTSToAnnual at line 469 which writes `oi != null ? { value: oi } : null`, NULL entries are pure null and get filtered. BUT canonical.annual.annualSBC / annualCapex are flat numbers, not wrapped. They are not in CRITICAL_FIELDS so _arrLen doesn't apply. The bug therefore primarily affects callers that re-wrap. Still, the branch `v === null || Number.isFinite(v)` is logically wrong: a non-finite-but-not-null value should never be 'present'.
findings.reproducer: Snapshot.annual.annualOpInc = [{value: null}, {value: null}, {value: null}, {value: null}]. CRITICAL_FIELDS check for 'annual.annualOpInc>=3' → _arrLen returns 4 → check passes → field counts as present, grade improves to A+ despite zero data.
findings.impact: Tickers with FTS-only Yahoo coverage (legacy data absent) can be over-graded as A+ when the data is actually fully null. The DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE gate gives them no cap.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 81 to `return Number.isFinite(v) || (v === null && typeof x === 'object' && !('value' in x));` — only allow null through when x is a balance-sheet-style object (has no 'value' key). Or simpler: filter `{value: null}` explicitly: `if ('value' in x && x.value == null) return false;`
findings.compound_hint: Combined with the price-only path which sets _quality.grade=null but leaves missing fields untouched, the same snapshot may oscillate between null-grade and stale-A+-grade across consecutive runs.

findings.title: field-coverage.isPresent treats array-of-nulls as covered
findings.description: isPresent returns true for any array with length > 0, even if every element is null. After the Bug-#26 fix in pull-yahoo.js, annualSBC and annualCapex contain null-padded year-aligned arrays. A ticker for which Yahoo returns no SBC data still yields annualSBC=[null,null,null,null,null], which isPresent considers a covered field. Coverage drift detection is therefore blind to a Yahoo schema change that converts data to all-nulls.
findings.mechanism: If Yahoo deprecates the 'stockBasedCompensation' field name in fundamentalsTimeSeries and the fallback name lookup fails, _ftsExtractByYear preserves null entries to keep year alignment. field-coverage counts the array as 100% covered. detectDrift compares 100% vs 100% → no drift.
findings.reproducer: Pull a universe where Yahoo returns annualSBC as [null,null,null,null,null] for every ticker. computeCoverage reports annual.annualSBC = 1.0 (100% covered). detectDrift returns no alert.
findings.impact: Schema-drift detector blind exactly where the Bug #26 fix preserved nulls. Methods (sbc-revenue, reinvestment-rate) silently produce garbage values because they receive null-only arrays.
findings.suggested_fix: Strengthen isPresent: for arrays, require at least one entry to be either a finite number, or an object containing a finite `.value`, or a balance-sheet-style object with at least one non-null property. Implementation: `if (Array.isArray(v)) return v.some(x => x != null && (typeof x === 'number' ? Number.isFinite(x) : (x.value != null && Number.isFinite(x.value)) || Object.values(x).some(p => p != null)));`
findings.compound_hint: Combines with F-DQ-004 — both functions need the same 'is this a real data point' predicate. Consider extracting a shared isMeaningful() helper used by data-quality, field-coverage, and downstream methods.

findings.title: Price-only fast-path bypasses MIN_MCAP filter
findings.description: The Tag-166 price-only fast-path returns early (line 694) when _priceOnlyUpdate succeeds — it never reaches the MIN_MCAP filter at line 822. A ticker that was above $1B at full-pull but has since dropped below (price decline, FX move, or share-count change) keeps its snapshot in the universe for up to FUNDAMENTALS_MAX_AGE_DAYS days. This is a stealth form of survivor bias: tickers don't leave the universe at the moment they cross the floor.
findings.mechanism: Day 0: full pull, mcap=$1.05B → pass. Day 3: price drops 10% → real mcap=$0.95B. Age < 7d → price-only path runs. q.marketCap written into existing.marketCap.value, snapshot saved, ticker stays. The MIN_MCAP guard would have evicted it on a full pull but the fast-path skips that check.
findings.reproducer: Create a snapshot with marketCap.value=1.05e9, age=2 days. Stub yf.quote() to return marketCap=0.5e9. Run processOne. The fast-path executes, writes mcap=0.5e9, returns 'price-only' status — no eviction, no filter applied.
findings.impact: Survivor-bias asymmetry: tickers can enter the universe instantly (full pull) but only leave it after fundamentals expire (7+ days). Cross-sectional rankings on day 3-6 include sub-$1B firms. Trade signals built on these snapshots are biased toward firms that recently lost mcap.
findings.suggested_fix: After _priceOnlyUpdate writes existing.marketCap.value, re-check MIN_MCAP/MAX_MCAP. If out of band, delete the snapshot (same code path as full-pull eviction) and record skipped-mcap. Alternatively, skip the price-only fast-path when |q.marketCap - existing.marketCap.value| / existing.marketCap.value > 0.15 — force a full pull on >15% mcap moves.
findings.compound_hint: Cross-check the _removed log (if any) — F-DQ-010 in the previous audit called for a graveyard.json; until that exists, this issue is invisible to retrospective analysis.

findings.title: FX_FALLBACK lacks 14 currencies that refresh-fx.js now fetches
findings.description: refresh-fx.js was expanded under F-DP-009 to fetch 14 additional currencies (PLN, TRY, THB, IDR, MYR, PHP, VND, CZK, HUF, RON, AED, SAR, QAR, ILS). But pull-yahoo.js's FX_FALLBACK still only contains the original 19 currencies. If fx-rates.json is missing, stale (>14d), or load-failed, all 14 newly-supported currencies fall through fxConversionFailed and their snapshots are skipped (line 811). Universe silently shrinks for emerging-markets exchanges whenever the FX refresh workflow is broken — exactly when the data is most needed for operators to debug.
findings.mechanism: loadFx returns without overlaying when ageDays > FX_STALE_DAYS. FX_TO_USD stays at FX_FALLBACK. _convertSnapshotToUSD looks up rate for, e.g., 'TRY' → undefined → fxConversionFailed=true → snapshot skipped. The whole Istanbul exchange disappears from the universe.
findings.reproducer: Backdate fx-rates.json fetchedAt to 30 days ago. Run pull-yahoo on a TRY-denominated ticker (e.g. BIMAS.IS). The fxConversionFailed branch fires, snapshot is skipped, the ticker is silently lost.
findings.impact: Fragile dependency on continuously-fresh fx-rates.json for 14 currencies. A single missed CI run >14 days ago drops large swaths of EM universe. No alert fires because skipped tickers count as 'skipped-mcap' / 'fx-unknown' — they don't fail the fail-ratio guard at line 1002.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend FX_FALLBACK with approximate 2025-current rates for all 14 new currencies (even if stale, better than dropping the snapshot). Or, change the stale fx-rates.json fallback policy to use the LAST known fx-rates.json rates rather than the hardcoded table — fx-rates.json stores per-currency lastSuccessAt, so individual currencies could be selected based on their own staleness instead of a global cutoff.
findings.compound_hint: Combined with F-DQ-003 — FX_FALLBACK merging is silent on a per-currency basis. Both findings argue for per-currency provenance tracking.

findings.title: Per-currency staleness (lastSuccessAt) is captured by refresh-fx but ignored by pull-yahoo
findings.description: refresh-fx.js writes per-currency currencyMeta with lastSuccessAt and lastAttemptAt (F-DP-010). pull-yahoo's loadFx reads only top-level fetchedAt to decide freshness — it ignores currencyMeta entirely. A currency that hasn't been successfully refreshed in 30 days but whose top-level fetchedAt is updated (because OTHER currencies succeeded) is treated as fresh. The intent of the per-currency tracking is silently defeated.
findings.mechanism: refresh-fx updates top-level fetchedAt as long as ANY currency succeeded this run. Top-level looks fresh. But e.g. JPY may have failed for 30 consecutive days (Yahoo currency endpoint frequently 404s on cross-rates). The stale JPY rate from 30 days ago is silently applied to every Japanese stock; no alert.
findings.reproducer: Construct fx-rates.json with fetchedAt = today, currencyMeta.JPY.lastSuccessAt = 30-days-ago, rates.JPY = 0.0067 (2024-stale). loadFx accepts the file (ageDays = 0 < 14). Snapshots for Japanese stocks get FX-converted with the stale rate. snap.meta.fxRateSource = 'fx-rates.json @ <today>' — misleading.
findings.impact: Silent currency-specific staleness undermines the entire per-currency-success design from F-DP-010. Operators believe per-currency provenance is enforced; it is not.
findings.suggested_fix: In loadFx, after parsing fx-rates.json, iterate currencyMeta and override individual rates with FX_FALLBACK (or null) when their lastSuccessAt is > FX_STALE_DAYS days old. Set FX_RATE_SOURCE_PER_CURRENCY map for downstream snapshot metadata.
findings.compound_hint: Aligned with F-DQ-003 / F-DQ-007 — all argue for per-currency rather than global FX provenance.

findings.title: check-pull-stats does not watch universeSize, yahooFailed, or fxFailed for drift
findings.description: collectStats() captures universeSize, yahooFailed, yahooSuccessRate, fxFailed — but the `watched` list at line 86 only includes ['yahooOk', 'fxRatesCount', 'earningsWithDate', 'priceTickerCount', 'snapshotsCount']. A universe shrinkage from 12000 → 9000 stocks (because refresh-universe.js dropped 25% of LSE tickers) would NOT trigger an alert. Similarly, a doubling of yahooFailed (Yahoo started rate-limiting more aggressively) is invisible to drift detection — only yahooOk matters, but if total universe size also shrank, yahooOk could even move UP while failures spiked.
findings.mechanism: watched watches DOWNWARD drift only and only on positive-counts metrics. universeSize is excluded. fxFailed is excluded (increase in failures = bad, but not watched). The drift detector therefore cannot alert on Yahoo schema changes that shift failures to the rate-limit class without changing the n_ok number.
findings.reproducer: Set today.universeSize = 8000 (vs historical median 12000, a 33% drop). detectStatsDrift returns []. No Discord alert. Operator only discovers the drop manually.
findings.impact: Survivor-bias detection is partially blind — universe-level shrinkage (the most direct survivor-bias signal) is not monitored. Combined with F-DQ-006, the system has multiple blind spots for universe attrition.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'universeSize' to watched. For yahooFailed and fxFailed, add upward-drift detection: if today's value is > median * (1 + threshold), alert. Or compute yahooFailRatio = yahooFailed / yahooTotal and watch that.
findings.compound_hint: watchlist.json size change ought to be the canonical proxy for universe attrition; pairing with F-DQ-006 (price-only path mcap leak) gives a fuller universe-stability picture.

findings.title: filingDate is always null — no defense against stale Yahoo TTM data
findings.description: meta.filingDate is hardcoded to null with a TODO comment 'Yahoo liefert kein Filing-Datum für TTM'. There is no other staleness check on the underlying financial data. A company whose Yahoo TTM revenue hasn't refreshed in 18 months (Yahoo blackout, delisting paperwork, regulatory hold) looks identical to a freshly-filed firm. meta.asOf reflects only when WE pulled, not when the data was filed. Stale-data detection is therefore impossible at the engine layer.
findings.mechanism: fundamentalsTimeSeries data carries period end-dates per row, but mapFTSToAnnual/mapFTSToQuarterly discard them. annualRev is `{value: ...}` — no date. revenueQ is `{value: ...}` — no date. The most-recent quarter's end-date is never stored. Two snapshots N months apart for the same firm are indistinguishable on staleness even if Yahoo froze data half a year ago.
findings.reproducer: Pull AAPL on day 0; pull again on day 30. If Yahoo's TTM didn't refresh (rare but happens), the two snapshots are byte-identical except for meta.asOf and meta.fetchedAt. There is no way to detect from the snapshot alone that the underlying data is frozen.
findings.impact: Earnings-stability and Q-over-Q methods compare values across snapshots, but cannot detect that the values came from the same Yahoo data dump pulled twice. Tickers in 'data-frozen' state masquerade as healthy.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract endDate / period dates from fundamentalsTimeSeries rows (Yahoo returns ISO timestamps per row). Store as meta.lastReportedPeriod = { annual: <date>, quarterly: <date> }. Add a freshness check: if lastReportedPeriod.quarterly is > 180 days old, flag the snapshot _staleFundamentals=true.
findings.compound_hint: Related to the absent fingerprint (F-DQ-012 in previous audit) — both findings argue for explicit content-freshness metadata. With period end-dates available, fingerprint can be derived as a side benefit.

findings.title: _priceOnlyUpdate nulls grade but leaves missingFields/nanRatio, leaking stale diagnostics
findings.description: F-DQ-009 (previous audit) was partially fixed at line 679: `if (existing._quality) existing._quality.grade = null`. But only grade is nulled — nanRatio, missingFields, and computedAt are preserved from the previous full-pull. Downstream code that reads _quality.nanRatio or iterates _quality.missingFields (e.g. scripts/data-quality-report.js) gets a mixed signal: grade=null suggests 'unknown', but nanRatio=0.1 + missingFields=[] suggests A+ quality. data-quality-report.js classifies these as 'unknown' (line 93 has 'unknown' bucket), but generate-modes-report.js falls back to `(s._quality && s._quality.grade) || null` — and any other consumer that uses nanRatio gets stale.
findings.mechanism: Partial nulling creates a partial-truth artifact: grade is null but the other fields look authoritative. Any analytic that bypasses .grade (e.g. a percentile sort by nanRatio) draws from stale data.
findings.reproducer: Snapshot has _quality = {grade:'A', nanRatio:0.05, missingFields:[], computedAt:'2026-01-01'} after full pull. Price-only path runs. _quality.grade becomes null. _quality.nanRatio is still 0.05. _quality.computedAt still says Jan 1. Downstream sees 'high-quality stale grade'.
findings.impact: Mixed-truth artifact in snapshot. Operators reading the JSON manually can't tell whether the grade is unknown-but-good or genuinely-unknown. Future code might use nanRatio without checking grade and misinterpret.
findings.suggested_fix: Either null the entire _quality block (`existing._quality = null` or `existing._quality = {grade: null, nanRatio: null, missingFields: [], computedAt: priceOnlyAt, stale: true}`), or re-run gradeSnapshot(existing) at the end of _priceOnlyUpdate so the grade is recomputed against the (still-stale) fundamentals. The latter is preferable — the fundamentals haven't changed, so the grade hasn't either, only the price/mcap did. Recomputing is cheap and consistent.
findings.compound_hint: Sibling to F-DQ-004 — both centre on _quality being trustworthy. Recomputing gradeSnapshot in _priceOnlyUpdate would also expose F-DQ-004 (NaN-counted-as-present) more readily.

findings.title: TRACKED_FIELDS still misses several method-critical paths
findings.description: F-DQ-006 (previous audit) added fcfMarginTTM, forwardPE, annualSBC, annualCapex, annualBalance, insiderOwnerPercent. Still missing: annual.annualRnD (Bug #25 added; used by reinvestment-rate), annual.annualOCF (Bug #23 added; used by ((Capex+R&D)/OCF) premium-compounder check #6), annual.annualNetIncome (used by many ROE/quality methods), timeseries.netIncomeQ (Tag-90 8-quarter-earnings-stability), insiderActivity.buyCount90d (insider-buy-cluster Tag-137), meta.firstTradeDate (IPO-year filters), metrics.priceSales (already partially tracked but only the .value).
findings.mechanism: If Yahoo silently stops returning insiderTransactions (e.g. SEC EDGAR sync glitch), insider-buy-cluster scores collapse to null. field-coverage drift wouldn't fire because insiderActivity.buyCount90d isn't tracked. Same logic for annualRnD: a Yahoo schema rename would silently zero out reinvestment-rate.
findings.reproducer: Mock yahoo.insiderTransactions = null across all tickers. canonical.insiderActivity is null. computeCoverage doesn't track this path → no drift alert. insider-buy-cluster method produces 0s — no signal.
findings.impact: Drift detector blind to several Bug-#23/#25/#26-added paths. Methods that depend on them silently degrade.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend TRACKED_FIELDS: 'annual.annualRnD', 'annual.annualOCF', 'annual.annualNetIncome', 'timeseries.netIncomeQ', 'insiderActivity', 'meta.firstTradeDate'. Consider deriving TRACKED_FIELDS from a single source of truth that also feeds CRITICAL_FIELDS (data-quality.js) — currently the two lists drift independently.
findings.compound_hint: Shared-truth opportunity: define a single FIELD_REGISTRY in a new file that both CRITICAL_FIELDS and TRACKED_FIELDS import. Eliminates the recurring 'someone added a field to one but not the other' pattern.

findings.title: No persistent fingerprint to detect frozen-data tickers across runs
findings.description: F-DQ-012 (previous audit) called for a fundamentalsFingerprint. Still absent. Each snapshot's meta has fetchedAt/asOf but no content hash. Two consecutive snapshots with identical fundamentals (Yahoo froze the data) are indistinguishable from two snapshots with genuinely-unchanged data (firm hasn't reported). With FUNDAMENTALS_MAX_AGE_DAYS=7 driving the price-only path, byte-level fundamentals can persist unchanged for an arbitrary number of price-only cycles, but there is no flag indicating 'this ticker's fundamentals haven't changed in N runs'.
findings.mechanism: Stale-data tickers blend into the universe undetected. Sector medians and percentile rankings include them as if they were live.
findings.reproducer: Pull a ticker twice with FUNDAMENTALS_MAX_AGE_DAYS=7 on consecutive days. The first run does a full pull; the second runs price-only. Both snapshots have different meta.asOf but identical annual.* and metrics.* — there is no fingerprint to detect this without a deep-diff.
findings.impact: Diagnostic friction; no easy way to alert on 'N runs with identical fundamentals'. Combined with F-DQ-010 (no filingDate), there is no structural defense against frozen-data tickers.
findings.suggested_fix: Compute meta.fundamentalsFingerprint = sha1(stringify({annualRev, annualOpInc, annualFCF, marketCap.value, metrics.revenueTTM.value})) at end of mapper. Add scripts/check-frozen-fundamentals.js that compares fingerprints across last 10 runs and lists tickers whose fingerprint hasn't changed in >180 days.
findings.compound_hint: Carry-over from previous audit. Bundled with F-DQ-010 for completion of the staleness-detection story.

findings.title: _log isHistoryUsable counts {totalCash: null, totalDebt: null, totalAssets: null} as usable
findings.description: The balance-override decision at line 784: `oldBalanceUsable = (canonical.annual.annualBalance || []).filter(r => r.totalDebt != null || r.totalCash != null || r.totalAssets != null).length`. This count is used to decide whether to REPLACE the quoteSummary balance with the FTS balance. But the mapper at line 318 already filters out rows where all three are null. So oldBalanceUsable equals canonical.annual.annualBalance.length always (every row has at least one non-null field). The override logic is effectively 'replace iff FTS has equal-or-more rows', which is not what the comment claims ('iff FTS has more nicht-null Werte').
findings.mechanism: The intended semantics — pick whichever source has more cells filled — is not what the code does. It picks whichever source has more ROWS where at least one cell is filled. If quoteSummary returns 5 rows × 1 field each, and FTS returns 4 rows × 3 fields each, quoteSummary wins (5 > 4) even though FTS has 12 cells vs quoteSummary's 5.
findings.reproducer: Set quoteSummary balance = 5 rows with only totalCash present (no debt or assets). Set FTS balance = 4 rows fully populated. oldBalanceUsable=5, newBalanceUsable=4. Override skipped. canonical.annual.annualBalance ends up with the inferior 5-row source.
findings.impact: Suboptimal balance-sheet source choice for a subset of tickers; mostly affects net-debt-ebitda and Altman-Z. Not catastrophic — the chosen source still has some data — but represents a silent loss of available data.
findings.suggested_fix: Count cell-level coverage: `usable = rows.reduce((sum, r) => sum + (r.totalCash != null) + (r.totalDebt != null) + (r.totalAssets != null), 0)`. Compare cell counts, not row counts.
findings.compound_hint: Minor data-quality optimization; defer to background cleanup pass.

findings.title: field-coverage history truncation can mask schema drift across HISTORY_WINDOW boundary
findings.description: updateHistory keeps only the last HISTORY_WINDOW=14 entries. computeBaseline averages all entries except the latest. If a schema drift has been silently persistent for >14 runs (e.g. Yahoo dropped a field 2 weeks ago and our 6/day cadence gives 84 entries — far more than 14), the baseline IS the post-drift state, so detectDrift never alarms. The ABSOLUTE_FLOOR check (F-DQ-005 in previous audit) catches this for fields below 50% — but for fields that settled at, say, 65% coverage post-drift, both the baseline check and the floor check pass.
findings.mechanism: Slow-onset drift: a field that gradually loses coverage from 95% → 90% → 85% → ... → 65% over 14 runs, with each individual run-over-run drop below DROP_THRESHOLD=0.20, never triggers an alert. After 14 runs, the baseline has fully absorbed the new normal at 65%, and the floor check (50%) is satisfied. The drop is now permanently invisible.
findings.reproducer: Construct 14 runs of fieldCoverage history where field X drops 2 percentage points per run (95→93→91→...→69). Drop per step = 0.02, well below DROP_THRESHOLD=0.20. No alert ever fires. After 14 runs, baseline ≈ 81% (avg of 95 down to 69), current 67%, drop 14pp — still below threshold.
findings.impact: Gradual schema drift over weeks goes undetected. Yahoo's actual deprecation pattern is sometimes gradual rolling rollouts, exactly matching this failure mode.
findings.suggested_fix: Maintain a long-term baseline (e.g. 90-day rolling) separate from the 14-run baseline. Compare current to long-term baseline with a smaller threshold (e.g. 10pp drop from 90-day vs current run). Or, track a 'high-water mark' per field and alert when current < 0.7 * highWaterMark.
findings.compound_hint: Combined with F-DQ-005 from previous audit (ABSOLUTE_FLOOR), the drift detector now catches sudden drops and floors, but not slow steady erosion.

findings.title: FX_STALE_DAYS=14 and refresh-fx STALE_WARN_DAYS=7 are inconsistent
findings.description: pull-yahoo treats fx-rates.json as fresh for 14 days. refresh-fx logs a STALE warning at 7 days. The two thresholds diverge by 2x. A snapshot consumed by pull-yahoo can be 13 days old (still 'fresh' per pull-yahoo) while refresh-fx warned 6 days ago that the same data was stale. Operators receive contradictory signals.
findings.mechanism: FX_STALE_DAYS in pull-yahoo.js line 95 = 14. STALE_WARN_DAYS in refresh-fx.js line 54 = 7. Configurable separately, not derived from a single constant.
findings.reproducer: Read both files. They reference the same fx-rates.json with different staleness cutoffs.
findings.impact: Operator confusion during FX incident triage. Minor; no direct data corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Move FX_STALE_DAYS to a shared config file (e.g. config/fx.json) and import in both scripts. Or set FX_STALE_DAYS = STALE_WARN_DAYS * 2 with an explicit comment about the warn-vs-hard-fail relationship.
